_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve represents Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, typically upward sloping, indicating higher yields for longer terms to compensate for risk and time. An inverted curve, where shorter-term yields exceed longer-term yields, has historically preceded all modern US recessions by 6 to18 months. Presently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.57% (maturing June 2026), with the 10-year minus 2-year spread (2s10s) at 0.37 percentage points, indicating a positive slope. This contrasts with the deepest recorded inversion at 0.0 points and the period from 2022 to 2024, when the curve was inverted.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a critical recession indicator as inversion often signals economic contraction ahead. The fact that the spread is now 0.37 points positive indicates a re-steepening after the curve remained inverted throughout 2022 to 2024. Despite this, the New York Fed's recession probability remains elevated at 31%, reflecting ongoing economic uncertainty. It is important to note that the yield curve frequently un-inverts shortly before recessions materialize, which may limit the lead time provided by the inversion signal.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve's positive 2s10s spread suggests diminished near-term recession risk compared to the recent inversion period. Nevertheless, the NY Fed's still substantial 31% recession probability advises continued vigilance, acknowledging that curve shape alone is not definitive and other economic factors play a role.
